jobs.description:
# Product Management, Research

Anthropic's mission centers on developing reliable, interpretable, and steerable AI systems that are safe and beneficial for users and for society.

Source: [https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/anthropic/jobs/(555) 555-0100](https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/anthropic/jobs/(555) 555-0100)
Date job was posted: 2026 (exact date not listed)

## About the Role

As a Product Manager for the Research team, you will guide the ideation and launch of new models and products advancing frontier AI technology. You will collaborate with research teams to transform applied research into products and uncover high-potential use cases grounded in customer needs. The role requires someone passionate about safe, beneficial AI who thrives in ambiguous settings and can identify non-obvious applications for emerging technologies.

## Responsibilities

- Lead vision, strategy, roadmap, and execution of frontier technologies solving real-world problems
- Partner with research to define, improve, and ship model capabilities
- Identify nascent research with transformative product potential
- Synthesize user insights into actionable requirements and evaluations
- Understand the AI landscape and market positioning
- Influence diverse stakeholders including research, product engineering, and platform engineering teams

## Qualifications

- Deep passion and regular engagement with AI and LLMs
- 5+ years in product management with experience launching new products and scaling existing ones
- First-principles thinking with ability to navigate ambiguity and find simple solutions
- Deep technical background with strong grasp of AI/ML concepts; Python and SQL proficiency
- Strong user empathy and ability to synthesize contradictory feedback
- Track record of launching ambitious products achieving distribution or commercial success
- Intellectual curiosity without ego; comfortable asking questions and learning independently
- Creative approach to technology risks and benefits
- Creative, hacker spirit and problem-solving orientation
- Bachelor's degree or equivalent combination of education, training, and professional experience

## Preferred Qualifications

- Not explicitly listed separately from required qualifications

## Compensation

- Annual Salary: $305,000-$385,000 USD
- Visa sponsorship available
- Hybrid: minimum 25% in-office time required
- Locations: San Francisco, CA or New York City, NY

---

jobs.cover_letter_md:
Dear Hiring Manager,

I spent four years at Florence Healthcare immersed in the clinical trial ecosystem, shadowing workflows at research sites, cataloging 250+ software vendors and 900+ products across the industry, and figuring out how fragmented systems could talk to each other. When I saw Ascertain's founding PM role focused on AI-driven integrations across healthcare providers, payers, and vendors, it felt like a natural next step for everything I have been building toward.

At Florence, I conducted over 200 customer discovery interviews including on-site workflow shadowing across 21+ product concepts, translating what I observed into structured specifications that engineering could act on. That research-first approach drove a 220% increase in feature adoption for one of our core products. I also managed M&A due diligence and technical integration for two major acquisitions, which gave me a sharp understanding of how to evaluate systems, data models, and integration architectures at a strategic level. On the go-to-market side, I led cross-functional strategy with sales and customer success, defined acceptance criteria and testing protocols, and launched a 0-1 product that shipped to two distinct market segments.

More recently, I have been deep in AI product work. At Curated For You, I designed agentic AI automation workflows that increased revenue per employee by 43%, built a conversational AI agent integrated with Slack, and created an MCP server (essentially an AI agent integration layer) that let users interact with our web application through natural language. At Omagine Labs, I co-founded a company and architected an AI pipeline using LLMs for behavioral pattern analysis. I am not a PM who hands off specs and waits. I prototype, I build, and I test hypotheses with working software. That combination of healthcare domain knowledge, hands-on AI building experience, and a founding mentality is what I would bring to Ascertain.

I would welcome the chance to talk about how my experience mapping healthcare vendor ecosystems and building AI integrations could accelerate Ascertain's strategy. I am available anytime for a conversation.

Jane
jobs.application_answers_text:
## Why are you interested in this role?

I've spent 4 years at Florence Healthcare building products in clinical trial technology, and the thing that consistently frustrated me was how much administrative work still runs on manual handoffs between disconnected systems. Ascertain's approach of using AI agents to automate those healthcare administrative workflows is exactly the problem space I want to be in. The founding PM role specifically appeals to me because I've done my best work building from zero, launching a 0-1 product at Florence, establishing a user research organization from scratch, and co-founding Omagine Labs. Owning integrations strategy across providers, payers, and vendors combines my healthcare domain knowledge with the AI agent and integration work I've been doing at Curated For You, where I built MCP servers and agentic automation workflows. This role sits at the exact intersection of my experience and my interests.

## Why do you want to work at Ascertain?

Ascertain's focus on AI-driven and AI-managed integrations resonates with me because I've seen firsthand how fragmented healthcare technology ecosystems are. At Florence Healthcare, I cataloged over 250 software vendors and 900+ products in clinical research, and the complexity of those vendor relationships and data flows is enormous, and automating the administrative layer between them is a massive unlock. What draws me to Ascertain specifically is the emphasis on customer discovery through workflow shadowing. That tells me the team values getting into the field and understanding real workflows before building, which matches how I operate. I conducted over 200 user interviews at Florence, many on-site at clinical research sites. I want to work with a team that builds products from deep customer understanding rather than assumptions, especially in a domain where getting workflows wrong has real consequences.

## Describe a challenging project or problem you solved.

At Florence Healthcare, I was tasked with launching a product that came to us through an acquisition, not a clean 0-1 build, but something that needed to be retooled and repositioned for two distinct market segments. The challenge was threefold: the acquired product had a different technical architecture, the original target market was narrow, and our sales team needed clear value propositions to sell into both segments. I led the cross-functional GTM strategy with sales and customer success, defining qualifying criteria and value propositions for each market. I implemented an end-to-end QA process with acceptance criteria and testing protocols for every release, which was critical because we were shipping to two different user bases with different workflow expectations. I also managed the technical due diligence and integration aspects of the acquisition itself. The result was a successfully launched product serving 2 separate markets, with a QA framework that became the standard process adopted across the product organization. This experience taught me how to evaluate integration architectures, navigate ambiguity in product direction, and ship under tight timelines with incomplete information.

## What makes you a strong candidate for this position?

Three things set me apart for this specific role. First, I bring 4 years of healthcare technology experience from Florence Healthcare combined with hands-on AI product work: I architected an AI pipeline leveraging LLMs at Omagine Labs and built agentic automation workflows that increased revenue per employee by 43% at Curated For You. That healthcare plus AI combination is rare in PM candidates. Second, I have direct integration-building experience: I created an MCP server enabling natural language interaction with a web application, consolidated isolated databases into unified systems, and built Node.js APIs, so I understand integration architecture from both the product and technical side. Third, my customer discovery depth matches exactly what Ascertain values: over 200 user interviews including on-site workflow shadowing, a user research organization I built from scratch, and a track record of translating those insights into specifications that drove 220% feature adoption increases.

## Is there anything else you'd like us to know?

My Biomedical Engineering degree from Georgia Tech gives me a foundational understanding of healthcare systems and clinical workflows that most PMs lack. Combined with my engineering background. I actively build full-stack applications and AI pipelines, not just spec them. I can go deep with engineering on integration architecture while maintaining the product judgment to prioritize which integrations drive the most scale. I'm also a Certified Scrum Product Owner.
jobs.review_text:
# Review: Founding Product Manager at Ascertain (Revision 2)

## Fabrication Check

Compared every bullet in `ascertain_founding_pm.tex` against `config/master_resume.tex`.

**No hard fabrications found.**

All tools, frameworks, metrics, and credentials trace back to the master resume:

- "43% revenue per employee" -- present in master (Curated For You)
- "220% feature adoption" -- present in master (Florence Healthcare)
- "200+ customer discovery interviews" -- present in master (Florence Healthcare, "200 user interviews")
- "250+ vendors, 900+ products" -- present in master (Florence Healthcare)
- "3,000+ registrations across 8 events" -- present in master (Florence Healthcare)
- "2 FTE employees" -- present in master (Curated For You)
- All tech stack items (React, Node.js, Supabase, PostgreSQL, Firebase, MongoDB, Langfuse, LangChain, Claude Code, MCP Servers) -- present in master
- CSPO certification -- present in master
- Georgia Tech BME + Entrepreneurship Certificate -- present in master

Keyword reframing and verb upgrades are all within encouraged bounds:
- "workflow shadowing" added to interviews bullet -- encouraged keyword matching
- "competitive analysis" framing of vendor catalog -- encouraged domain synonym
- "acceptance criteria and testing protocols" framing of QA work -- encouraged keyword matching
- "customer-obsessed," "bias toward action," "systems thinking" in summary -- encouraged soft skill additions
- "Architected" replacing "Developed" -- encouraged verb upgrade

**Verdict: CLEAN -- no fabrication issues.**

---

## Keyword Coverage

Based on the Keyword Map from the analysis:

### Hard Skills (PRESENT in master)
| Keyword | Status |
|---------|--------|
| Product management | COVERED (summary, title, bullets) |
| Product strategy | COVERED (skills section) |
| B2B SaaS | COVERED (summary) |
| AI agents / AI-driven / AI-powered | COVERED (summary, Omagine, Curated bullets) |
| Integrations / API integrations | COVERED (summary, skills "AI & Integrations") |
| User research / customer discovery | COVERED (summary, skills, multiple bullets) |
| Workflow shadowing | COVERED (Florence Sr. IBA bullet, line 182) |
| 0-1 product development | COVERED (summary, skills, Omagine bullet, Florence bullet) |
| Cross-functional collaboration | COVERED (Florence GTM bullet, Curated mentorship bullet) |
| Go-to-market / GTM strategy | COVERED (Omagine bullet, Florence bullet) |
| Acceptance criteria | COVERED (Florence QA bullet) |
| Testing protocols / QA process | COVERED (Florence QA bullet) |
| Metrics tracking / customer feedback | COVERED (Florence 220% bullet, Early Access Programs bullet) |
| Roadmap prioritization | COVERED (skills section, Florence Sr. IBA bullet) |
| Competitive analysis | COVERED (Florence Sr. IBA bullet, line 183) |
| Technical specifications | MISSING -- "translating complex workflows into scalable product solutions" is close but not explicit |
| Node.js, React, Supabase, PostgreSQL | COVERED (skills section) |
| LLM / AI pipeline | COVERED (Omagine bullet, skills "LLM Pipelines") |
| Healthcare technology / clinical research | COVERED (Florence section, summary, presentations) |

### Hard Skills (GAP -- not in master)
| Keyword | Status | Note |
|---------|--------|------|
| curl | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |
| Postman | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |
| HL7 | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |
| FHIR | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |
| EDI | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |
| HIPAA | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |
| SOC 2 | MISSING | Genuine gap -- acceptable |

### Soft Skills
| Keyword | Status |
|---------|--------|
| Customer-obsessed | COVERED (summary) |
| Bias toward action | COVERED (summary) |
| Systems thinking | COVERED (summary) |
| Executive communication | MISSING |
| Mentorship | COVERED (Curated bullet) |
| Leadership | COVERED (co-founder title, "led" verbs) |
| Ambiguity tolerance | MISSING |
| Strategic prioritization | COVERED (Florence Sr. IBA bullet) |
| High-leverage thinking | MISSING |
| Scale-oriented mindset | COVERED (summary "scalable product solutions") |

**Coverage: 18/19 present-in-master keywords covered = 95%**

Only "technical specifications" is missing and addressable. The 7 GAP keywords are genuine skill gaps and do not count against the score. Soft skill misses (executive communication, ambiguity tolerance, high-leverage thinking) are minor.

---

## ATS Risk Flags

1. **Location field empty on job entries** -- The `\jobEntry` commands have empty fourth parameters. Some ATS systems extract location from job entries. Not a blocker since the header has "NYC" but worth noting.
2. **Florence role progression via `\roleEntry`** -- Sub-roles within one company entry may confuse some ATS parsers that expect one title per company. Minor risk.
3. **Role date fields empty for Sr. IBA and IBA** -- Some ATS systems expect date ranges for each role. Minor risk.
4. **No other ATS issues** -- standard template with `\pdfgentounicode=1`, clean section headers, no graphics, comma-separated skills.

**Overall ATS risk: LOW**

---

## Content Alignment Score

**8/10**

This resume is sharply targeted at Ascertain. The summary explicitly positions the candidate at the intersection of healthcare technology + AI + B2B SaaS + integrations, which is the exact Venn diagram for this role. Florence Healthcare provides rare healthcare domain credibility that most PM candidates cannot match. The Curated For You bullets (MCP server, agentic AI workflows, Slack integration) map almost directly to "AI agents that automate administrative work." The 0-1 framing across Omagine and Florence matches the "founding PM" signal. The summary includes "customer-obsessed," "bias toward action," and "systems thinking" -- direct posting language.

What keeps it from a 9-10:
- The resume has 21 bullets when the blueprint specified exactly 18. Three extra bullets dilute focus: "Researched clustering algorithms" (too narrow/technical for a PM role), "Launched 2 Early Access Programs" (not in blueprint), and "Created and launched an Interactive Clinical Trial Technology Landscape" (redundant with the competitive analysis bullet above it).
- Five presentation entries when the blueprint called for exactly one. The four extra entries waste space without adding keyword signal.
- The page is almost certainly too dense or overflowing given the extra content.

---

## Strongest Bullets

1. **"Increased revenue per employee by 43% by designing and deploying agentic AI automation workflows"** (Curated For You) -- This is the single best bullet for Ascertain. It combines a hard metric with "agentic AI automation workflows," which is Ascertain's entire business model. A hiring manager building AI agents for healthcare automation would stop here.

2. **"Conducted 200+ customer discovery interviews including on-site workflow shadowing across 21+ product concepts"** (Florence Sr. IBA) -- Hits three posting keywords in one bullet: "customer discovery," "workflow shadowing," and the implicit "translating complex processes into specifications." The volume (200+ interviews, 21+ concepts) signals genuine customer obsession at scale.

3. **"Created MCP server enabling users to interact with the web application directly through natural language"** (Curated For You) -- MCP servers are AI agent integration infrastructure. This is the closest analog to what Ascertain builds: integration layers between AI agents and existing systems. A technical CPO will immediately see the relevance.

---

## Weakest Bullets

1. **"Researched clustering algorithms to automatically map product embeddings across vendor catalogs"** (Curated For You) -- The blueprint explicitly excluded this bullet. It reads as a data science side project, not PM work. For an integrations-focused PM role, this adds no signal about customer workflows, healthcare, or AI agent automation.
   - **Recommendation:** Remove entirely per the blueprint. This brings Curated to 6 bullets as planned.

2. **"Created and launched an Interactive Clinical Trial Technology Landscape for market intelligence"** (Florence Sr. IBA) -- This duplicates the competitive analysis bullet directly above it (250+ vendors, 900+ products). Both describe the same project. Having both makes the Florence section feel padded.
   - **Recommendation:** Remove. The competitive analysis bullet already covers this work with better specifics and keyword alignment.

3. **"Launched 2 Early Access Programs, including sales strategy, implementation, and customer feedback sessions"** (Florence Sr. IBA) -- Not in the blueprint's final selection. While it shows GTM involvement, the Florence PM section already has a stronger GTM bullet. This is generic early-access launch work that does not differentiate for an AI/integrations PM role.
   - **Recommendation:** Remove to hit the 18-bullet target. GTM credibility is already established.

---

## One-Page Check

**LIKELY FAILING.** The resume has 21 bullets (blueprint planned 18) and 5 presentation entries (blueprint planned 1). This is significantly more content than the blueprint's space budget, which was calculated as a "tight but achievable" one-page fit at 18 bullets with 1 presentation. The extra 3 bullets and 4 presentation entries almost certainly push the resume past one page or eliminate all whitespace. Must compile to verify after cuts.

---

## Overall Verdict

**MINOR REVISIONS**

No fabrication issues. Keyword coverage is excellent at 95%. The resume is well-targeted. However, the drafter deviated from the blueprint's content budget, adding 3 extra bullets and 4 extra presentation entries.

### Changes needed (priority order):

1. **Cut Presentations to 1 entry** ("AI in Clinical Trials" only) as the blueprint specified. Remove the other 4 entries.
2. **Remove 3 extra bullets** to hit the 18-bullet target:
   - Remove "Researched clustering algorithms" (Curated For You) -- blueprint excluded it
   - Remove "Created and launched an Interactive Clinical Trial Technology Landscape" (Florence Sr. IBA) -- redundant with competitive analysis bullet
   - Remove "Launched 2 Early Access Programs" (Florence Sr. IBA) -- not in blueprint, generic
3. **Compile to PDF and verify one-page fit** with acceptable whitespace (0-5pt target) after the above cuts.
4. **Optional improvement:** Sharpen the summary phrase "translating complex workflows into scalable product solutions" to "translating complex workflows into structured technical specifications" to cover the one missing addressable keyword.

jobs.title: Technical Product Manager II, Data Platforms
jobs.description:
# Technical Product Manager II, Data Platforms

The New York Times Data Platform team seeks to empower the organization to access and use data to answer important questions, power user experiences, and to make strategic decisions. This technical product manager position focuses on developing analytics, business intelligence tools, and related experiences.

Source: https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/thenewyorktimes/jobs/(555) 555-0100
Date job was posted: N/A
Hiring manager (if available): N/A

## About the Role

Technical Product Manager II on the Data Platform team at The New York Times. Hybrid role based in New York, NY. Focuses on developing coordinated data product roadmaps, building analytics solutions, and enabling discovery and consumption of content and business data.

## Responsibilities

- Develop coordinated data product roadmaps with data leaders
- Build high-quality, user-friendly data experiences and analytics solutions
- Enable discovery and consumption of content and business data
- Create curated data experiences that address business needs
- Partner cross-functionally to understand and prioritize analytics needs
- Deliver iterative products with stakeholder feedback loops
- Measure impact using qualitative and quantitative methods
- Foster advanced visualization adoption with power users

## Qualifications

- 4+ years designing analytics, BI solutions, or data products in a product management capacity
- Proven track record implementing complex data systems with data analysis and optimization
- Hands-on experience with data handling approaches and technologies
- Knowledge of data governance, modeling, pipelines, and infrastructure
- Ability to communicate technical concepts to diverse audiences

## Preferred Qualifications

- Background in software development, engineering, data science, or marketing tech
- Experience with tools like Hex, dbt, MonteCarlo, or Chartbeat
- Familiarity with SQL, Python, BigQuery, AWS, or Spark

## Compensation

- Base Salary: $120,000-$142,000 USD
- Variable pay (bonus/stock)
- Medical/dental/vision coverage, 401(k) matching, paid leave, parental leave, tuition reimbursement, professional development

---
jobs.match_explanation: Mid-level technical PM at major NYC media company.
jobs.cover_letter_md:
Dear Hiring Manager,

I am writing to apply for the Technical Product Manager II role on the Data Platform team at The New York Times. At Curated For You, I centralized isolated databases and built an in-app dashboard that automated the work of two full-time employees -- that experience of turning fragmented data into usable internal tools is exactly the kind of work your team is doing at a much larger scale.

Over the past five years, I have built a product management career grounded in understanding what users actually need and then shipping solutions that deliver measurable results. At Florence Healthcare, I increased feature adoption by 220% through research and roadmap prioritization, conducted over 200 user interviews across 21+ product ideas, and launched two Early Access Programs with structured feedback sessions to iterate quickly. I also cataloged and categorized over 250 software vendors and 900+ products in clinical research, then turned that dataset into an interactive map that increased marketing website traffic by over 2.5x. That project -- taking a large, messy dataset and building a curated, user-friendly experience on top of it -- maps directly to the kind of data product work your team does for analysts, scientists, and product managers across the NYT.

Beyond the PM fundamentals, I bring hands-on technical depth. I have built full-stack applications using React, Node.js, PostgreSQL, Python, and Supabase, and I have deployed services on AWS EC2. I am not writing your data pipelines, but I can read the code, understand the architecture, and have informed conversations with engineering teams about prioritization and tradeoffs. I have also presented at eight industry conferences and hosted eight virtual summits, so translating technical concepts for diverse audiences is something I do regularly and well.

The opportunity to build internal data experiences that help the entire NYT organization make better decisions is genuinely compelling to me. I would welcome the chance to discuss how my experience building data products and running user research programs could contribute to the Data Platform team.

Jane
jobs.application_answers_text:
# Application Answers: Technical Product Manager II, Data Platforms -- The New York Times

## Why are you interested in this role?

I am drawn to this role because it sits at the intersection of product management and data infrastructure -- two areas where I have direct experience. At Curated For You, I centralized isolated databases and built an in-app dashboard that automated the work of two full-time employees, which gave me firsthand appreciation for how well-designed data products can transform how an organization operates. The emphasis on building user-friendly data experiences, partnering across the organization to identify analytics requirements, and implementing iterative solutions with rapid feedback cycles aligns closely with how I work. I have built user research programs from scratch, conducted over 200 user interviews, and consistently used both qualitative and quantitative methods to measure impact and prioritize roadmaps. The opportunity to apply these skills to empower NYT's analysts, scientists, and product managers with better data tools is compelling.

## Why do you want to work at The New York Times?

The New York Times has executed one of the most successful digital transformations of any legacy media company, and the Data Platform team is a core enabler of that ongoing evolution. What appeals to me is the mission behind this team -- empowering the organization to leverage data for answering critical questions and driving strategic decisions. That is a product challenge that requires deep understanding of internal users, careful stakeholder management across multiple domains, and thoughtful, evidence-based product development. I thrive in environments where product decisions are grounded in research and measured impact rather than gut instinct. Having built research organizations from scratch and delivered measurable outcomes like 220% feature adoption increases, I want to bring that discipline to a company whose work genuinely matters to informed public discourse.

## Describe a challenging project or problem you solved.

At Florence Healthcare, I was tasked with cataloging and categorizing over 250 software vendors and 900+ products across the clinical research technology landscape -- a space with no existing centralized resource. The challenge was not just the volume of data but making it useful to diverse stakeholders including sales teams, product managers, and external customers. I designed the categorization framework, collected and organized the data, and then created an interactive map published at researchrevolution.com that made the entire landscape navigable. The result increased traffic to the marketing website by over 2.5x, becoming a key asset for sales conversations and industry positioning. Separately, this work directly informed product strategy -- it helped me identify whitespace opportunities that led to launching a 0-1 product from ideation through acquisition and retooling to two separate markets. The project demonstrated my ability to take a large, unstructured dataset and turn it into a curated data experience that delivered measurable business value across multiple teams.

## What makes you a strong candidate for this position?

Three specific qualifications make me well-suited for this role. First, I have direct experience building internal data tools -- at Curated For You, I centralized isolated databases and developed an in-app dashboard, which mirrors the data platform consolidation and self-serve analytics work this role focuses on. Second, I bring deep user research expertise, having conducted over 200 user interviews and built research programs from scratch, which maps directly to partnering across the organization to identify and address analytics requirements. Third, I have a hands-on technical background -- I have built full-stack applications using React, Node.js, PostgreSQL, Supabase, and Python, and deployed services on AWS (EC2), giving me the technical fluency to collaborate effectively with engineering teams and make informed prioritization decisions about data products.

## Is there anything else you'd like us to know?

My engineering degree from Georgia Tech combined with hands-on software development experience means I can speak the language of the data and engineering teams I would partner with daily. I have also presented at eight industry conferences and hosted numerous virtual summits, which reflects my ability to communicate technical concepts to diverse audiences -- a skill this role explicitly requires.
jobs.review_text:
# Review: Technical Product Manager II, Data Platforms -- The New York Times

**Revision Cycle**: 1

---

## Fabrication Check

The previous review's major flags -- systematically swapping "AI-powered" to "data-driven," rebranding "interactive map" as "interactive data experience," and disguising the AI pipeline as a data pipeline -- have all been corrected. The resume now preserves honest language throughout. Good work on that front.

However, new issues were introduced in this revision:

**FLAG 1 (FABRICATION) -- Florence PM Bullet 3: "Led assimilation of an acquisition, coordinating with engineering teams through iterative delivery"**

The master resume says: "Lead assimilation of an acquisition and moved to product team after transition." The tailored version appends "coordinating with engineering teams through iterative delivery." Neither "coordinating with engineering teams" nor "iterative delivery" appear in the master resume in connection with this work. The master resume summary does say "collaborate effectively with engineering teams," but that is a general self-description, not a claim about what happened during the acquisition assimilation. This bullet grafts two job-posting keywords ("engineering teams," "iterative") onto an experience that the master resume does not describe that way. This is fabrication through embellishment.

**FLAG 2 (FABRICATION) -- Summary: "cross-functional delivery"**

The master resume uses "cross-functional collaboration" (in the mentoring bullet). The tailored summary says "cross-functional delivery." "Collaboration" and "delivery" mean different things. The master resume does not claim "cross-functional delivery" anywhere. This phrase was manufactured to hit the "delivery" keyword.

**FLAG 3 (BORDERLINE) -- Summary: "qualitative and quantitative user interviews"**

The words "qualitative" and "quantitative" do not appear anywhere in the master resume. The blueprint argues this is supportable because interviews are inherently qualitative and the candidate tracked quantitative metrics. That reasoning is defensible but it is still inserting job-posting keywords into a description where the source material does not use those words. Recommend softening: describe the user research and metric tracking separately rather than stamping "qualitative and quantitative" onto "user interviews."

**FLAG 4 (BORDERLINE) -- Skills: "SQL" as standalone skill**

The master resume lists PostgreSQL but never lists SQL separately. PostgreSQL implies SQL usage, but the master resume does not make this claim explicitly. Acceptable if the candidate confirms they write raw SQL queries; worth noting as a borderline addition.

**CLEARED from prior review:**
- "AI-powered" language preserved throughout -- FIXED
- "interactive map" preserved (not changed to "interactive visualization") -- FIXED
- "AI pipeline" preserved with LLM references -- FIXED
- "agentic" removed from automation workflow bullet -- acceptable simplification
- "isolated databases" preserved in summary (not changed to "data sources") -- FIXED
- "interactive data experience" reverted to honest language -- FIXED

**Fabrication verdict: 2 clear flags (Flags 1 and 2), 2 borderline flags (Flags 3 and 4).**

---

## Keyword Coverage

Based on the Keyword Map from analysis.md:

| Keyword | Status |
|---|---|
| product roadmaps | COVERED -- "roadmap prioritization" in summary, Omagine bullet 3, Florence Sr. BA bullet 1 |
| product manager | COVERED -- job titles |
| cross-functional | COVERED -- summary, mentoring bullet |
| engineering teams | COVERED -- Florence PM bullet 3 (but flagged as fabrication) |
| prioritization | COVERED -- summary, multiple bullets |
| delivery | COVERED -- summary "cross-functional delivery" (but flagged as fabrication) |
| iterative | COVERED -- Florence PM bullet 3 (but flagged as fabrication) |
| feedback cycles | COVERED (partial) -- "feedback sessions" in Early Access bullet |
| qualitative and quantitative | COVERED -- summary (but flagged as borderline) |
| user-friendly | MISSING |
| data experiences | MISSING (acknowledged gap) |
| analytics | MISSING |
| product roadmap | COVERED -- via "roadmap prioritization" |
| technical concepts | MISSING |
| diverse audiences | MISSING |
| software development | COVERED -- "Software Developer" job title |
| SQL | COVERED -- skills section |
| Python | COVERED -- Om Coach tech stack and skills section |
| AWS | COVERED -- skills section "AWS (EC2)" |
| data products | MISSING (acknowledged gap) |
| product strategy | COVERED -- summary, Omagine bullet 3 |
| visualization | MISSING -- "interactive map" does not use the word |

**Raw coverage: 15 out of 22 keywords = 68%**

Excluding keywords for genuine gaps the candidate cannot honestly claim (data experiences, data products, analytics as a domain): 15 out of 19 = 79%

However, 3 of those 15 "covered" keywords (engineering teams, delivery, iterative) are covered only through fabricated text. If those are removed pending fixes: 12 out of 19 = 63%.

**Easy wins still on the table:** "user-friendly," "technical concepts," "diverse audiences," and "visualization" are all honestly supportable but absent from the resume. The Presentations section implicitly demonstrates communicating technical concepts to diverse audiences, but the words never appear. The Interactive Landscape bullet uses "interactive map" when "interactive visualization" would arguably be acceptable (a map is a type of visualization, though the prior review flagged this -- the candidate should decide).

---

## ATS Risk Flags

1. **Location fields empty for all jobs**: The `\jobEntry` commands pass empty strings for the location parameter. Some ATS systems expect location data for each position. Low risk.

2. **"Languages & Data" skills category name**: Slightly unconventional. ATS systems generally parse skills sections regardless of category names, so this is cosmetic.

3. **Em-dashes as double hyphens**: The `--` notation renders as en-dashes in the PDF. Standard and ATS-safe.

4. **pdfgentounicode enabled**: Good -- ensures text extraction works for ATS parsing.

5. **No critical ATS risks identified.**

---

## Content Alignment Score

**6/10**

This revision is more honest than the prior version, which is the right trade-off. The resume now reads as "strong PM with technical chops and some data-adjacent experience" rather than trying to cosplay as a data platforms person. The database centralization and dashboard bullet is well-positioned as the lead Curated For You entry. The Interactive Landscape project demonstrates data curation. The Om Coach metrics extraction bullet shows comfort with analytics concepts.

The core problem remains: the candidate does not have data platform, BI, or analytics product management experience, and the analysis correctly identified this as a significant gap. The resume does the best it can with available material. A 6 reflects that this is a well-crafted resume for a role where the candidate has meaningful experience gaps in the core requirements. This is an alignment ceiling issue, not a resume quality issue.

---

## Strongest Bullets

1. **"Centralized isolated databases and developed an in-app dashboard to automate the work of 2 FTE employees"** -- The single most relevant bullet to the data platforms role. It directly demonstrates database consolidation and internal dashboard work. The 2 FTE impact quantifies value. A hiring manager reading this would think "this person has touched data infrastructure and built internal tools." It maps directly to the posting's emphasis on building user-friendly data experiences and solutions.

2. **"Increased feature adoption by 220% through research and roadmap prioritization"** -- Massive quantified impact tied to the exact PM skills the role demands. For an internal data platform where adoption is the key success metric, showing you can drive 220% adoption increases is compelling. The combination of research and prioritization mirrors the role's emphasis on understanding user needs and coordinating delivery.

3. **"Conducted and analyzed over 200 user interviews across 21+ new product ideas"** -- Volume demonstrates systematic user research capability. Maps directly to "partner across the organization to identify and address analytics requirements." The breadth across 21+ product ideas shows the candidate can context-switch across domains -- relevant for a data platform PM who serves multiple business units.

---

## Weakest Bullets

1. **"Mentored a junior designer, providing guidance on product thinking and cross-functional collaboration"** -- This is filler. It exists to place the "cross-functional collaboration" keyword, but a data platform PM hiring manager does not care about junior designer mentoring. The keyword already appears in the summary.
   - **Suggested action**: Cut this bullet. Use the reclaimed space to add a line about communicating technical concepts to diverse audiences (supported by the 8+ conference presentations in the master resume), or simply let the page breathe.

2. **"Developed AI pipeline to ingest and analyze meetings with LLMs for behavioral pattern analysis"** -- Honestly preserved from the master resume (good), but this bullet is misaligned with the data platforms role. It signals AI/ML expertise rather than data infrastructure understanding. A hiring manager may wonder why the candidate is applying for a data platforms role when their recent work is in AI pipelines.
   - **Suggested action**: No rewrite possible without fabrication. Consider condensing Omagine to 2 bullets (keep bullets 1 and 3, cut this one) to save space for more aligned content.

3. **"Led assimilation of an acquisition, coordinating with engineering teams through iterative delivery"** -- Beyond the fabrication flag, this bullet is vague even with the added details. What was assimilated? What was the scope? A hiring manager cannot picture the work.
   - **Suggested rewrite**: Revert to honest language from the master resume: "Led assimilation of an acquisition and transitioned to product team after completion." If space allows, add a second Florence PM bullet that naturally demonstrates cross-functional engineering coordination from actual experience. If no such bullet exists in the master resume, accept the keyword gap.

---

## One-Page Check

Cannot directly verify PDF page count from this review. Based on content volume (14 experience bullets, 5 project bullets, 4 skills lines, 2 education lines, 2 presentation entries), this should fit on one page with the current margins. The blueprint trimmed Presentations from 4 to 2 entries, which helps significantly.

**Recommendation**: Verify by running `scripts/compile_latex.sh nyt_tpm_data_platforms` and checking page count output.

---

## Overall Verdict

**MAJOR REVISIONS**

The prior review's fabrication-through-reframing issues (AI-to-data swaps) have been fixed. However, 2 new fabrication flags were introduced by embellishing the Florence acquisition bullet and manufacturing "cross-functional delivery" in the summary. These must be corrected before shipping.

### Changes needed, in priority order:

1. **Fix Florence PM Bullet 3**: Remove "coordinating with engineering teams through iterative delivery." Revert to master resume language: "Led assimilation of an acquisition and transitioned to product team." If "engineering teams" and "iterative" keywords are needed, find an honest home for them or accept them as missing. The master resume summary does say "collaborate effectively with engineering teams" -- this could be echoed in the tailored summary if not already there.

2. **Fix Summary "cross-functional delivery"**: Change to "cross-functional collaboration" to match the master resume, or simply say "cross-functional product delivery" only if the candidate's experience genuinely includes owning delivery across functions (not just collaborating).

3. **Soften "qualitative and quantitative" in summary**: Rephrase to avoid stamping job-posting keywords onto claims the master resume does not make with those words. For example: "200+ user interviews and building research programs from scratch" is already strong without the keyword injection. Alternatively, split it: "qualitative user research including 200+ interviews" and mention "quantitative impact measurement" separately near the metrics.

4. **Add missing easy-win keywords honestly**: "Technical concepts" and "diverse audiences" can be added via a phrase in the summary like "communicating technical concepts to diverse audiences" -- directly supported by 8+ conference presentations in the master resume. "User-friendly" could modify "data experiences" or "in-app dashboard" if natural.

5. **Cut the mentoring bullet** and consider cutting the AI pipeline bullet to make room for more aligned content or improve page density.

jobs.title: Technical Product Manager, App Messaging Platforms
jobs.description:
# Technical Product Manager, App Messaging Platforms at The New York Times

The New York Times seeks a Technical Product Manager to lead messaging capabilities across iOS and Android apps, supporting the company's digital subscription growth mission.

Source: https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/thenewyorktimes/jobs/(555) 555-0100
Date job was posted: Not specified
Hiring manager (if available): Reports to Senior Technical Product Manager, Onsite Messaging & Pricing Platforms

## About the Role

This position supports the company's growth mission by accelerating the digital subscription business. You'll partner with product managers, cross-functional stakeholders, and engineering leadership to develop engagement and subscription features across NYT brands including News, The Athletic, Cooking, Games, and Wirecutter.

## Responsibilities

- Own the product roadmap for iOS and Android messaging platforms, aligned with enterprise goals
- Collaborate with engineering, data, and stakeholders to identify platform opportunities and form product requirements
- Balance business needs, technical requirements, and user experience when prioritizing work
- Analyze user data and KPIs to identify improvements and measure impact on subscription growth
- Demonstrate commitment to journalistic independence and the company's mission

## Qualifications

- 2+ years in product management, focusing on platform products within iOS/Android ecosystems
- Experience launching and iterating large-scale features across full product development cycles
- Data-driven approach to inform product decisions and improve system performance
- Strong cross-functional communication and stakeholder collaboration skills
- Agile product development methodology experience

## Preferred Qualifications

- Enthusiasm for solving technical challenges and leading transformational projects
- Experience analyzing complex systems and using data to identify improvements
- Familiarity with subscription business models and media industry technologies

## Compensation

- Base Salary: $104,000 - $125,000 USD annually
- Additional Benefits: Variable pay eligibility (bonuses, restricted stock), medical/dental/vision, 401(k) matching, paid time off, parental leave, tuition reimbursement
- Location: New York, NY (Hybrid, 2 days/week in office)
- Requisition ID: REQ-019339

---
jobs.match_explanation: Mid-level TPM role at NYT focused on mobile app messaging, low experience bar (2+ years) and subscription growth focus.
jobs.cover_letter_md:
Dear Hiring Manager,

I'm applying for the Technical Product Manager, App Messaging Platforms role at The New York Times. Over the past five years, I've built my career around platform product management -- owning roadmaps, shipping 0-1 products, and using user research to drive prioritization -- and most recently I built and launched an iOS app that scaled to 250 users in its first two months. The combination of mobile platform work and data-driven product management maps directly to what this role requires.

At Florence Healthcare, I progressed from Business Analyst to Product Manager over four years, owning the full product development cycle from ideation through release. I increased feature adoption by 220% through research-driven roadmap prioritization, conducted over 200 user interviews across 21+ product ideas, and launched two Early Access Programs that included sales strategy, implementation, and user feedback sessions. That work required constant cross-functional collaboration with engineering, sales, and leadership to balance business needs against technical constraints and user experience -- exactly the kind of stakeholder coordination this role demands across NYT's brands. I also hold a Certified Scrum Product Owner certification, which grounds my approach to Agile product development.

On the mobile side, I built GOOFY ATL, a full-stack iOS social media app using React Native and Node.js, with features like location-based discovery, in-app 3D tours, and peer sharing. Managing a mobile product end-to-end -- from architecture decisions to a launch event to scaling users -- gave me direct experience with the iOS ecosystem and the engagement mechanics that matter for in-app platforms. Earlier, at Georgia Tech, I ran the official @georgiatech social media accounts across Snapchat and Instagram, which was my first exposure to managing messaging and engagement on mobile platforms at scale for a large institution.

I'm drawn to this role because it sits at the intersection of platform product work and user engagement in service of a mission I respect. I'd welcome the chance to discuss how my experience could contribute to the messaging platforms team. I'm available to talk anytime.

Jane
jobs.application_answers_text:
## Why are you interested in this role?

I'm drawn to this role because it sits at the intersection of platform product management and user engagement at scale, which maps directly to my experience. Owning a product roadmap for messaging platforms across iOS and Android is the kind of cross-functional, technically grounded work I've been doing throughout my career. At Florence Healthcare, I owned product roadmaps, increased feature adoption by 220% through research and prioritization, and launched Early Access Programs that required balancing business needs, technical constraints, and user experience. I also built and launched a React Native iOS app that scaled to 250 users, giving me hands-on familiarity with the iOS ecosystem. The opportunity to apply these product management fundamentals to drive digital subscription growth across NYT brands like News, The Athletic, Cooking, Games, and Wirecutter is genuinely compelling.

## Why do you want to work at The New York Times?

The New York Times' commitment to journalistic independence while simultaneously building a world-class digital subscription business is a rare combination. I'm motivated by the challenge of building messaging platform products that serve both user experience and business growth without compromising editorial integrity. The multi-brand ecosystem -- spanning News, The Athletic, Cooking, Games, and Wirecutter -- means this role requires managing competing priorities from multiple product teams, which is something I developed at Florence Healthcare where I conducted 200+ user interviews across 21+ product ideas and organized a 100+ attendee user conference. I also managed official social media accounts for Georgia Tech across Snapchat and Instagram, which gave me experience thinking about engagement and messaging on mobile platforms in an institutional, media-adjacent context.

## Describe a challenging project or problem you solved.

At Florence Healthcare, I was tasked with increasing adoption of a feature that was underperforming despite significant engineering investment. The challenge was that the product team had built based on assumptions rather than validated user needs, and stakeholders were resistant to changing direction on something they had already committed resources to. I conducted extensive user interviews as part of the 200+ interviews I led across 21+ new product ideas, using those insights to build a data-driven case for reprioritizing the roadmap. I worked cross-functionally with engineering and sales to reframe the feature's value proposition and adjust the implementation approach. Through this research-driven roadmap prioritization, I increased feature adoption by 220%. This experience taught me that measurable product impact comes from disciplined user research and the ability to communicate data-backed recommendations to cross-functional stakeholders -- skills I would bring directly to analyzing KPIs and prioritizing platform improvements at NYT.

## What makes you a strong candidate for this position?

Three specific qualifications make me well-suited for this role. First, I have 5+ years of product management experience including roadmap ownership, cross-functional collaboration with engineering and sales, and launching 0-1 products through full development cycles at Florence Healthcare. Second, I bring hands-on iOS platform experience from building a React Native iOS app that I launched and scaled to 250 users, giving me practical understanding of the mobile ecosystem this role operates in. Third, I hold a Certified Scrum Product Owner (CSPO) certification, directly addressing the Agile methodology requirement. My data-driven approach -- demonstrated by conducting 200+ user interviews and delivering a 220% feature adoption increase -- aligns with the role's emphasis on analyzing user data and KPIs to drive product decisions.

## Is there anything else you'd like us to know?

I managed official Georgia Tech social media accounts across Snapchat and Instagram, which gave me direct experience with mobile messaging and engagement platforms in an institutional context. Combined with building a social media iOS app that scaled to 250 users, I bring a practical understanding of how users interact with messaging and content on mobile that complements my product management fundamentals.
jobs.review_text:
# Review: Technical Product Manager, App Messaging Platforms -- The New York Times

## Fabrication Check

Every bullet in the tailored resume was compared line-by-line against `config/master_resume.tex`.

**FLAG 1 -- Omagine Labs Bullet 1: "cross-platform communication coaching product"**
The master resume says "AI-powered meeting communication coach." The tailored resume changed "AI-powered meeting" to "cross-platform," which introduces a claim not present in the master resume. The master resume never describes this product as "cross-platform." This is a fabrication -- the word "cross-platform" implies multi-OS support that is not stated in the source material. The Om Communication Coach project in the master resume describes a "MacOS desktop app," which is the opposite of cross-platform.

**FLAG 2 -- Curated For You Bullet 1: "an automation workflow"**
The master resume says "agentic automation workflow." Dropping "agentic" is a minor softening, which is acceptable reframing (removing a buzzword, not adding one). **No flag -- acceptable.**

**FLAG 3 -- Florence Sr. Analyst Bullet 3: "sales strategy, implementation, and feedback sessions"**
The master resume says "sales strategy/calls, implementation, and feedback sessions." Dropping "/calls" is a minor omission. **No flag -- acceptable.**

**FLAG 4 -- Innovations Business Analyst Bullet 2: "discovery and sales"**
The master resume says "discovery interviews and sales." Dropping "interviews" is a minor shortening. **No flag -- acceptable.**

**FLAG 5 -- Summary: "revenue per employee by 43%"**
The summary says "revenue per employee by 43%" which is an awkward rephrasing of the master resume's "Increased revenue per employee by 43%." The claim itself is sourced. The phrasing is just clumsy (missing the verb "increasing"). **No fabrication, but a grammar issue.**

All other bullets trace cleanly to the master resume. Skills listed all appear in the master resume. Metrics (220%, 43%, 200+, 250 users, 2.5x, 3,000+, 250 vendors, 900+ products) all match source material.

**Summary: 1 fabrication flag (cross-platform claim in Omagine bullet).**

---

## Keyword Coverage

Keywords from the analysis Keyword Map:

| Keyword | Status |
|---------|--------|
| product roadmap | COVERED (summary: "roadmap prioritization") |
| product requirements | MISSING -- not explicitly stated anywhere |
| cross-functional stakeholders | COVERED (summary, skills, mentoring bullet) |
| engineering leadership | MISSING -- not explicitly stated |
| user experience | MISSING -- not explicitly stated as a phrase |
| user data | COVERED (implicit via "200 user interviews," "data-driven") |
| KPIs | MISSING -- not stated anywhere |
| product management | COVERED (summary: "Product manager") |
| platform products | COVERED (summary: "platform products") |
| full product development cycles | COVERED (implied by "ideation through release," "0-1 product") |
| data-driven | COVERED (summary: "data-driven product decisions") |
| product decisions | COVERED (summary) |
| cross-functional communication | COVERED (skills, mentoring bullet) |
| stakeholder collaboration | COVERED (skills: "Cross-functional Collaboration") |
| Agile | COVERED (CSPO certification implies Agile) |
| product development methodology | MISSING -- not explicitly stated |
| technical challenges | MISSING -- not explicitly stated |
| complex systems | MISSING -- not explicitly stated |
| iOS | COVERED (GOOFY ATL project, summary) |

**Coverage: 12/19 = 63%**

The missing keywords (product requirements, engineering leadership, user experience, KPIs, product development methodology, technical challenges, complex systems) are all terms the candidate could honestly use but chose not to include. These are not skill gaps -- they are missed optimization opportunities. "KPIs" and "product requirements" in particular should be easy to weave in.

---

## ATS Risk Flags

1. **Summary grammar issue**: "including 220% feature adoption increases and revenue per employee by 43%" reads awkwardly. It should say "and 43% revenue-per-employee gains" or similar. An ATS won't care, but a human reader will stumble.
2. **Em dashes in summary**: The LaTeX `--` renders as en/em dashes. Most ATS systems handle this fine, but some older systems may not parse them well.
3. **No issues with section headers**: All standard section names (Summary, Professional Experience, Education, Skills, Technical Projects) are ATS-friendly.
4. **Presentations section at the bottom is fine**: It won't confuse parsers.
5. **Overall ATS risk: LOW.** The resume uses a clean LaTeX template with standard formatting.

---

## Content Alignment Score

**7/10**

Justification: The resume does a solid job of positioning the candidate as a platform-oriented PM with mobile experience. The GOOFY ATL iOS app is prominently featured, the social media accounts bullet in Education is a smart inclusion for a messaging platform role, and the summary effectively frames the candidate around platform products and data-driven decisions. However, the resume still reads somewhat as a general PM resume rather than one laser-targeted at a messaging/engagement platform role. The word "messaging" does not appear anywhere. The word "engagement" does not appear. The subscription growth angle is entirely absent (acknowledged as an unfillable gap). The Presentations section adds minimal value for this specific role and takes up space that could be used for stronger alignment. The Interactive Clinical Trial Technology Landscape project, while showing data organization skills, is only loosely connected to this role.

---

## Strongest Bullets

1. **"Increased feature adoption by 220% through research and roadmap prioritization"** -- This is the single most relevant metric for a role focused on driving subscription growth through messaging features. It directly demonstrates the candidate can move engagement numbers through product decisions.

2. **"Launched app with event in May of 2025 in Atlanta and scaled to 250 users in 2 months"** -- For a role requiring iOS platform experience, this bullet proves the candidate has actually shipped a mobile product and grown its user base. The fact that it is a social media app (peer sharing, engagement features) makes it doubly relevant to a messaging platform role.

3. **"Ran official @georgiatech social media accounts across Snapchat and Instagram"** -- This is a non-obvious but excellent inclusion. It directly demonstrates experience managing messaging and engagement on mobile platforms in a media-adjacent institutional context. A hiring manager at NYT would immediately see the parallel.

---

## Weakest Bullets

1. **"Implemented thorough QA process from ideation through release for every line of code"** -- This reads as a generic process bullet. For a TPM role focused on messaging platforms and subscription growth, QA process ownership is table stakes, not a differentiator. **Suggested rewrite**: Replace with a bullet that demonstrates balancing business needs with technical requirements, or analyzing data to inform prioritization -- both of which are explicit responsibilities in the job posting. If no source material supports such a bullet, consider cutting this and adding a bullet from elsewhere.

2. **"Cataloged and categorized over 250 vendors and 900+ products in clinical research technology"** -- While it shows data organization at scale, it reads as a research/analysis task with no clear connection to messaging platforms or mobile product management. **Suggested rewrite**: If keeping this project, lead with the impact bullet ("Created interactive map increasing traffic to marketing website by over 2.5x") and cut the cataloging bullet. Better yet, if space is tight, consider replacing this entire project section with the "Organized a 100+ attendee user conference in Atlanta" bullet under Florence Sr. Analyst, which better demonstrates stakeholder management.

3. **"Mentored a junior designer, providing guidance on product thinking and cross-functional collaboration"** -- Mentorship is nice but generic. It does not differentiate the candidate for this specific role. **Suggested rewrite**: This is a low-priority bullet. If space is needed for more targeted content, cut it first from Curated For You.

---

## One-Page Check

CONFIRMED. The PDF is exactly one page. Content fills the page well with the Presentations section anchoring the bottom.

---

## Overall Verdict

**MAJOR REVISIONS**

The fabrication flag on "cross-platform" in the Omagine Labs bullet requires correction before this can ship.

**Changes needed, in priority order:**

1. **Fix fabrication**: Change "cross-platform communication coaching product" back to "AI-powered meeting communication coach" or a faithful variant. Do not claim cross-platform unless the master resume supports it.
2. **Fix summary grammar**: "and revenue per employee by 43%" should read "and 43% revenue-per-employee gains" or "increasing revenue per employee by 43%."
3. **Add missing high-value keywords**: Work "KPIs" and "product requirements" into existing bullets. For example, the Florence PM bullet about GTM strategy could mention "product requirements" and the 220% bullet could reference "KPIs."
4. **Consider replacing the QA bullet** with something more aligned to the role's emphasis on balancing business needs, technical requirements, and user experience -- if a source bullet exists in the master resume to support it.
5. **Evaluate whether the Presentations section earns its space**. It adds little for this role. That space could allow adding the "Organized a 100+ attendee user conference" bullet back into Florence, which better demonstrates stakeholder collaboration at scale.
jobs.notes: Posting closed - no longer accepting applications
